question_frequency.rationale: The Pair asks one genuine question at the end of Turn 2 ('Which direction interests you?') and a soft follow-up about next problems. There are no didactic checkpoint questions during the actual problem-solving — the entire solution is delivered as a lecture, with no moments inviting the user to predict, derive, or choose.

question_quality.rationale: The single substantive prompt is a meta-question about which next Euler problem to tackle, not about the crux of #15 itself (e.g., why C(40,20) and not C(40,20)+C(40,20), the Pascal identity derivation, or the space-optimization trade-off). The O(n) space refinement is presented as fait accompli rather than as a question to surface.

scaffolding.rationale: There is essentially no scaffolded sequence — Turn 1 dumps the full solution (DP, closed-form, equivalence proof, final answer all at once), and Turn 2 adds the 1D optimization without building toward it. No difficulty gradient, and answers are revealed before any prompts.

flow_and_tone.rationale: Tone is warm and the prose is polished, with clean tables and a coherent narrative. But it reads as monologue rather than dialogue; the user's role devolves into rubber-stamping. The closing question is natural but lands after all teaching is finished.

effectiveness.rationale: The experiment delivers the correct answer 137,846,528,820 from the start (the baseline initially wrote 640 and had to be corrected by the user). It also adds the O(n) space optimization and a clean Pascal identity derivation, making it slightly stronger than baseline on content quality.

friction.rationale: Experiment ran ~314s vs baseline's ~53s (roughly 6× longer) across 2 turns vs 1. The extra length brought a correct first answer and the 1D space optimization, but little of the added time produced pedagogical interaction — most of the overhead is additional exposition, not teaching gains.
